units.title: 📎 Supplementary Notes (3/24/2026)
units.content:
📝 Notes

📜 Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide

Exam: March 27, 2026 | 60 Objective Questions + DBQ + SAQ | Created by Mojo Jojo 🐵

📝 UNIT 1: COLONIAL AMERICA

Columbian Exchange & Early Exploration

Columbian Exchange: Transfer of plants, animals, diseases, culture

between Old & New Worlds after 1492. Devastated Native populations

via disease (smallpox).

Treaty of Tordesillas (1494): Spain & Portugal divided New

World; Spain got most of Americas.

Black Legend: Propaganda portraying Spanish as uniquely cruel to

Natives; used by English/Dutch to justify their own colonization.

Protestant Reformation: Led by Martin Luther; challenged

Catholic Church; motivated religious migration to Americas.

Spanish Armada (1588): England defeated Spain's fleet → opened

Atlantic to English colonization.

Joint Stock Company: Investors pool risk/profit; funded Virginia

Company → Jamestown (1607).

Colonial Founding & Development

Barbados Slave Codes (1661): First formal slave laws; brought to

Carolina; defined enslaved as property.

Mayflower Compact (1620): First self-government doc in

America; Pilgrims agreed to majority rule.

"City upon a Hill" (1630): Winthrop's speech; Puritan vision of

model Christian community in Massachusetts.

Anglo-Powhatan Wars (1610–1646): Conflicts between English

settlers and Powhatan Confederacy in Virginia.

King Philip's War (1675–76): Wampanoag (Metacom/King Philip)

vs. colonists; bloodiest colonial war per capita; Natives lost.

New England Confederation (1643): First attempt at colonial

cooperation for defense against Natives & Dutch.

Colonial Economy & Society

Triangular Trade: Manufactured goods → Africa → slaves →

Americas → raw materials → Europe.

Bacon's Rebellion (1676): Virginia frontiersmen vs. Governor

Berkeley; burned Jamestown; led planters to prefer enslaved Africans

over indentured servants.

Salutary Neglect: Britain loosely enforced trade laws → colonies

developed self-governance & smuggling habits.

New France: French colonies in Canada; focused on fur trade; good

Native relations; few settlers compared to British.

Road to the French & Indian War

Ohio River Valley: France & Britain competed for control →

sparked French & Indian War (1754–63).

Albany Congress (1754): Franklin's Plan of Union proposed

colonial cooperation; rejected by colonies & Crown.

Battle of Quebec (1759): British victory; decisive battle ending

French power in North America.

Proclamation of 1763: Britain forbade colonists from settling west

of Appalachians; colonists furious → fueled resentment.

📝 Causes of the Revolution

Mercantilism: Colonies exist to enrich the mother country; raw

materials out, manufactured goods in; colonies can only trade with

Britain.

Stamp Act (1765): First direct tax on colonists (paper goods);

colonists: "No taxation without representation!" → Stamp Act

Congress formed.

Boston Massacre (1770): British soldiers killed 5 colonists; used as

propaganda by Patriots (Paul Revere's engraving).

Boston Tea Party (1773): Sons of Liberty dumped 342 chests of tea

→ Parliament passed Intolerable Acts.

Intolerable Acts (1774): Closed Boston Harbor, revoked MA self-

government, forced housing of troops → united colonies against

Britain.

First Continental Congress (1774): Delegates met; agreed to

boycott British goods; not yet seeking independence.

📝 The War for Independence

Battle of Bunker Hill (1775): Technically British win but costly;

showed colonists could fight.

Olive Branch Petition (1775): Last attempt at reconciliation with

King George; rejected.

Common Sense (1776): Thomas Paine; argued for independence in

plain language; widely read.

Declaration of Independence (1776): Jefferson; natural rights

(Locke); listed grievances; "all men are created equal."

Battle of Saratoga (1777): Turning point; convinced France to ally

with Americans.

Treaty of Paris (1783): Britain recognized U.S. independence; U.S.

got land to Mississippi River.

British strengths: trained army, navy, Loyalist support, $$$. Colonial

strengths: home turf, guerrilla tactics, Washington's leadership, French

alliance.

📝 3/22/26, 2:55 PM

Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide

file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html

1/7

📝 UNIT 3: THE EARLY REPUBLIC

Articles of Confederation → Constitution

Articles of Confederation: 1st U.S. gov't. Strengths: Northwest

Ordinance, Land Ordinance. Weaknesses: No power to tax, no

executive, no regulate commerce, required unanimous votes to amend.

Land Ordinance of 1785: Divided NW Territory into townships for

sale; funded public education (Section 16).

Northwest Ordinance (1787): Outlined path to statehood; banned

slavery in NW Territory; protected civil liberties.

Great Compromise: Bicameral Congress: Senate (2/state) + House

(population-based).

Three-Fifths Compromise: Enslaved counted as 3/5 of a person

for representation & taxation.

Electoral College: Compromise for indirect presidential election;

small states get more weight.

📝 Ratification Debate

Federalists: Supported Constitution; strong central government;

Hamilton, Madison, Jay.

Anti-Federalists: Opposed Constitution; feared tyranny; wanted Bill

of Rights first.

Federalist Papers: 85 essays by Hamilton, Madison, Jay defending

Constitution. Key: #10 (factions), #51 (checks & balances).

Bill of Rights (1791): First 10 Amendments; added to win Anti-

Federalist support for ratification.

Hamilton's Economic Program

Financial Plan: Assumed state debts, created national bank, tariffs

for industry, excise taxes.

Bank of the United States: Hamilton's plan; Jefferson opposed

(strict vs. loose interpretation of Constitution).

Whiskey Rebellion (1794): Farmers refused to pay whiskey tax;

Washington sent militia → showed federal power.

Washington & Adams Era

Neutrality Proclamation (1793): Washington stayed neutral in

France vs. Britain war.

Jay's Treaty (1795): Settled issues with Britain; controversial;

critics said U.S. gave too much.

Washington's Farewell Address (1796): Warned against political

📝 parties, permanent alliances, sectionalism.

Alien & Sedition Acts (1798): Adams; extended citizenship wait;

made it illegal to criticize government → Democratic-Republicans

outraged.

VA & KY Resolutions (1798–99): Madison & Jefferson; states can

nullify unconstitutional federal laws (nullification theory).

📝 Federalist

Democratic-Republican

Strong central gov't

States' rights

📝 Strict interpretation

Pro-British

Pro-French

Commerce & manufacturing

📝 Jefferson Era

Election of 1800: "Revolution of 1800" — peaceful transfer of

power; Jefferson beat Adams; Burr tie led to 12th Amendment.

Marbury v. Madison (1803): Marshall established judicial review

— Supreme Court can strike down laws. Most important SCOTUS

case.

Louisiana Purchase (1803): Doubled U.S. size for $15M from

Napoleon; Jefferson stretched strict interpretation.

Embargo Act (1807): Jefferson banned all exports to hurt Britain &

France; backfired — hurt American economy.

Battle of New Orleans (1815): Jackson's great victory —

technically after Treaty of Ghent ended War of 1812.

Hartford Convention (1814–15): Federalists opposed War of

1812; proposed amendments; ended their party when war ended well.

Era of Good Feelings & Monroe

Tariff of 1816: First protective tariff; protected American industry

from British competition.

American System (Clay): Protective tariffs + national bank +

internal improvements (roads/canals).

Missouri Compromise (1820): Missouri = slave, Maine = free;

36°30' line divided future states.

Monroe Doctrine (1823): Western Hemisphere closed to European

colonization; U.S. would not interfere in Europe.

Election of 1824 / Corrupt Bargain: No majority → House

decided; Clay gave votes to J.Q. Adams; Adams made Clay Sec. of

State → "corrupt bargain."

📝 3/22/26, 2:55 PM

Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide

file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html

2/7

📝 Jackson Era

Election of 1828: Jackson won; "Age of the Common Man";

expanded voting rights (white men only).

Spoils System: Rewarding political supporters with government jobs

("rotation in office").

Nullification Crisis (1832): SC nullified Tariffs of 1828 & 1832

("Tariff of Abominations"); Jackson threatened force; Compromise of

1833 resolved it.

Bank War: Jackson vetoed recharter of Bank of U.S.; called it

"monster" favoring the rich; withdrew fed funds to state "pet banks."

Indian Removal Act (1830): Forced Native tribes west of

Mississippi; Trail of Tears (1838) — Cherokee forced march; ~4,000

died.

Cultural & Reform Movements

Erie Canal (1825): Connected Great Lakes to NYC; made NY

commercial hub; transportation revolution.

Second Great Awakening: Religious revival; inspired reform

movements (abolitionism, temperance, women's rights).

Seneca Falls Convention (1848): First women's rights

convention; Declaration of Sentiments ("all men and women are

created equal"); led by Stanton & Mott.

Free Soil Party (1848): Opposed spread of slavery to new

territories; "Free Soil, Free Speech, Free Labor, Free Men."

Abolition Movement: Garrison (The Liberator), Frederick Douglass

(escaped slave/speaker), Harriet Tubman (Underground Railroad).

Slavery Justifications: Necessary Evil → Peculiar Institution

→ Positive Good (evolution of Southern defense of slavery).

📝 UNIT 5: EXPANSION & THE PUSH TOWARD WAR

Manifest Destiny & Mexican-American War

Whig Party / John Tyler: Formed against Jackson; Tyler (Whig

VP) vetoed Whig agenda → expelled from party.

Manifest Destiny: Belief God destined Americans to expand across

continent; justified expansion/displacement of Natives.

Mexican-American War (1846–48): Texas annexation dispute;

U.S. gained CA, NM, AZ, NV, UT, CO from Mexico (Mexican Cession).

Wilmot Proviso (1846): Proposed banning slavery in any territory

gained from Mexico; passed House, failed Senate; intensified sectional

crisis.

Compromise of 1850: CA = free state; popular sovereignty in

NM/UT; stronger Fugitive Slave Law; ended DC slave trade.

Ostend Manifesto (1854): Secret plan to buy/seize Cuba for

slavery; leaked → outrage in North.

Gadsden Purchase (1853): Bought southern AZ/NM from Mexico

for railroad route.

📝 Road to Civil War

Popular Sovereignty: Let settlers in each territory vote on slavery;

proposed by Douglas; pleased neither side.

Underground Railroad: Network of safe houses helping enslaved

escape to North/Canada; Harriet Tubman made 19 trips.

Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854): Repealed Missouri Compromise;

popular sovereignty in KS & NE → "Bleeding Kansas."

Uncle Tom's Cabin (1852): Harriet Beecher Stowe; novel

humanizing enslaved people; radicalized Northern opinion against

slavery.

Bleeding Kansas (1856): Pro- & anti-slavery settlers fought in

Kansas; violence previewed Civil War.

Dred Scott Decision (1857): SCOTUS: enslaved people = property,

not citizens; Congress cannot ban slavery in territories; enraged

North.

Harper's Ferry (1859): John Brown's raid on federal arsenal to

spark slave revolt; captured, executed; made him martyr in North,

villain in South.

Election of 1860: Lincoln won without a single Southern electoral

vote; South saw it as signal to secede.

Southern Secession: SC left first; 11 states formed Confederate

States of America; said states had right to leave.

📝 UNIT 6: THE CIVIL WAR

Strengths, Strategies & Key Events

North

South

Larger population, industry

Defensive war, home turf

RR network, navy

📝 Strong military leadership

$$$, more factories

"King Cotton" diplomacy

King Wheat (food for Europe)

Fighting spirit/morale

Border States: Slave states that stayed in Union (MO, KY, MD, DE);

Lincoln kept them through careful politics.

Anaconda Plan: Union strategy: blockade South, control

Mississippi, squeeze Confederate economy.

Draft / NYC Draft Riots (1863): Poor workers (esp. Irish

immigrants) rioted against draft; could pay $300 to avoid service —

"rich man's war, poor man's fight."

Key Documents & Battles

Emancipation Proclamation (1863): Freed enslaved in

Confederate states only; war aim now included ending slavery;

prevented European recognition of Confederacy.

Gettysburg Address (1863): Lincoln's speech redefining war as

fight for equality and democracy.

Battle of Antietam (1862): Bloodiest single day; tactical draw but

Union "victory" → gave Lincoln cover to issue Emancipation

Proclamation.

Battle of Gettysburg (1863): Turning point in East; Lee's invasion

of North repelled; 50,000+ casualties over 3 days.

Battle of Vicksburg (1863): Union captured last Confederate

stronghold on Mississippi → split Confederacy in two.

📝 3/22/26, 2:55 PM

Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide

file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html

3/7

📝 UNIT 7: RECONSTRUCTION

Plans & Politics

Lincoln's 10% Plan: South could return when 10% swore loyalty

oath; moderate approach.

Johnson's Requirements: Similar to Lincoln's but added large

landowner pardon requests; lenient toward ex-Confederates.

Wade-Davis Bill (1864): Required 50% loyalty oath; pocket-vetoed

by Lincoln; showed Radical Republican desire for harsher terms.

Military Reconstruction Act (1867): Divided South into 5

military districts; required new state constitutions + ratify 14th

Amendment to rejoin.

Moderates vs. Radicals: Moderate Republicans wanted quick

reunion; Radicals (Sumner, Stevens) wanted to protect Black rights &

punish South.

Amendments, Resistance & End

Black Codes: Southern laws restricting freedmen's rights; forced

labor, limited movement; essentially re-enslaved many.

Freedmen's Bureau: Federal agency providing food, education,

legal aid to formerly enslaved people.

13th Amendment (1865): Abolished slavery.

14th Amendment (1868): Citizenship for all born in U.S.; equal

protection; due process.

15th Amendment (1870): Cannot deny vote based on race.

Redeemer Governments: Southern Democrats regained control;

ended Reconstruction reforms.

Jim Crow Laws: Racial segregation laws in South; "separate but

equal."

KKK: Founded 1865; terrorized Black voters, Republicans, freedmen

through violence.

Plessy v. Ferguson (1896): SCOTUS upheld "separate but equal"

→ legalized segregation for 58 years until Brown v. Board.

📝 UNIT 8: THE GILDED AGE & INDUSTRIALIZATION

Politics & Reform

Waving the Bloody Shirt: Republicans blamed Democrats for Civil

War to win elections.

Compromise of 1877: Hayes became president; federal troops left

South → ended Reconstruction.

Pendleton Act (1883): Created civil service exam; ended patronage

for federal jobs; merit-based hiring.

Thomas Nast: Political cartoonist; exposed Boss Tweed/Tammany

Hall corruption; invented Republican elephant & Democratic donkey.

Grant Admin Corruption: Crédit Mobilier, Whiskey Ring scandals;

though Grant himself likely not corrupt.

Interstate Commerce Act (1887): First federal regulation of

railroads; created ICC.

Sherman Anti-Trust Act (1890): First federal law breaking up

monopolies; rarely enforced until TR.

Wabash v. Illinois (1886): States cannot regulate interstate

railroad rates → need for federal regulation.

Industry, Labor & Immigration

Vertical Integration (Carnegie): Control all stages of production

(steel: mines → mills → railroads).

Horizontal Integration (Rockefeller): Control same stage across

industry (buy out all oil refineries).

Old Immigrants: Pre-1880, W. Europe (British, German, Irish);

Protestant; assimilated more easily.

New Immigrants: Post-1880, S/E Europe (Italian, Polish, Jewish,

Russian); Catholic/Jewish; faced more discrimination; Nativist

backlash.

Homestead Strike (1892): Carnegie Steel vs. workers; Pinkerton

agents hired; workers lost; set back unions.

Pullman Strike (1894): Nationwide railroad strike; Cleveland sent

federal troops; Eugene Debs arrested → showed government sided

with business.

📝 Western Expansion

Homestead Act (1862): 160 acres free to settlers who farmed 5

years; drew millions West.

Railroad Development: Transcontinental RR (1869) connected

nation; Chinese & Irish labor; transformed economy.

Dawes Severalty Act (1887): Broke up tribal lands; gave Native

families 160 acres; destroyed tribal culture; took 90M acres from

Natives.

Farmers' Problems: Falling prices, railroad monopolies, debt; led

to Grange movement and Populist Party.

The Grange / Farmers' Alliance: Farmers' organizations; pushed

for railroad regulation, currency reform.

Populist Party & Election of 1896

Populist (People's) Party: Demanded: direct election of senators,

graduated income tax, government ownership of railroads, silver

coinage.

Election of 1896: Bryan (D/Populist) vs. McKinley (R); Bryan's

"Cross of Gold" speech demanded silver coinage to inflate prices and

help debtors; McKinley won; end of Populism.

📝 3/22/26, 2:55 PM

Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide

file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html

4/7

📝 Rise of Imperialism

Isolation → Imperialism: Mahan's "sea power" theory; Social

Darwinism; need for markets; influenced by European empires.

Annexation of Hawaii (1898): American planters overthrew

Queen Liliuokalani; Cleveland refused annexation; McKinley annexed

it.

Spanish-American War (1898): USS Maine explosion; yellow

journalism; Cuba/Philippines; "Splendid Little War"; U.S. became

imperial power.

Treaty of Paris (1898): Spain gave U.S. Philippines, Guam, Puerto

Rico for $20M; Cuba became independent (nominally).

Platt Amendment (1901): U.S. retained right to intervene in Cuba;

Cuba could not sign treaties without U.S. approval → virtual

protectorate.

Pacific & Latin American Policy

Open Door Notes (1899–1900): Secretary Hay; demanded equal

trading rights in China for all nations; protected China from full

colonization.

Philippine Rebellion (1899–1902): Filipinos (led by Aguinaldo)

fought U.S. occupation; brutal war; contradicted American ideals.

Roosevelt Corollary (1904): Extension of Monroe Doctrine; U.S.

📝 would intervene in Latin American affairs to maintain order

("international police power").

Panama Canal (1914): TR supported Panamanian revolution vs.

Colombia; U.S. built canal; transformed global trade.

📝 UNIT 10: THE PROGRESSIVE ERA

Roosevelt's Progressivism

Progressive Movement: Reason: fix problems of industrialization.

Goals: break monopolies, protect consumers, expand democracy.

Supporters: middle class, muckrakers, women.

Square Deal: TR's program: control corporations, consumer

protection, conservation of natural resources (3 C's).

The Jungle (1906): Upton Sinclair exposed meatpacking industry

horrors → Meat Inspection Act & Pure Food & Drug Act.

Anthracite Coal Strike (1902): TR mediated; first time gov't sided

with workers (sort of); both sides arbitrated.

Northern Securities Case (1904): TR busted railroad monopoly

using Sherman Anti-Trust Act → "trust-busting."

Newlands Act (1902): Federal money from land sales used for

irrigation in West; conservation effort.

Muller v. Oregon (1908): SCOTUS upheld law limiting women's

work hours; used sociological data (Brandeis Brief).

Taft, Wilson & Progressive Amendments

TR vs. Taft: TR hand-picked Taft; Taft disappointed TR with

conservative moves; TR ran again as Bull Moose.

New Nationalism (TR) vs. New Freedom (Wilson): TR:

regulate big business but keep it. Wilson: break up all trusts, restore

competition.

Bull Moose Party (1912): TR's third party; split Republican vote →

Wilson won.

Wilson's "Triple Wall of Privilege": Attacked tariffs, banks, trusts

via: Underwood Tariff (lowered rates), Federal Reserve Act (central

banking), Clayton Anti-Trust Act (strengthened Sherman).

16th Amendment: Federal income tax.

17th Amendment: Direct election of senators (previously by state

legislatures).

18th Amendment: Prohibition (ban on alcohol).

19th Amendment: Women's suffrage.

📝 UNIT 11: WORLD WAR I

U.S. Entry & Key Events

Lusitania (1915): German U-boat sank British liner killing 1,198

incl. 128 Americans; outraged U.S. public.

Sussex Pledge (1916): Germany promised not to sink passenger

ships without warning after Sussex attack.

Fourteen Points (1918): Wilson's peace plan: freedom of seas, self-

determination, League of Nations; idealistic.

Schenck v. United States (1919): SCOTUS upheld limits on free

speech during wartime; "clear and present danger" test; upheld

Espionage Act.

Treaty of Versailles (1919): Blamed Germany (War Guilt Clause);

$33B reparations; stripped German territory; humiliated Germany →

seeds of WWII.

League of Nations: Wilson's pet project; Senate rejected U.S.

membership (Lodge Reservations); Article X concerned U.S.

sovereignty.

Lodge Reservations: Senator Lodge's conditions for joining

League; mainly about preserving Congressional war powers; Wilson

refused compromise.

Home Front & Aftermath

Committee on Public Information: Gov't propaganda bureau;

stirred anti-German sentiment.

Espionage & Sedition Acts: Criminalized opposition to the war;

suppressed civil liberties.

Great Migration: Black Americans moved North for industrial jobs;

escaped Jim Crow.

📝 3/22/26, 2:55 PM

Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide

file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html

5/7

📝 Social Tensions

Red Scare (1919–20): Fear of communist revolution after Russian

Revolution; Palmer Raids; Sacco & Vanzetti.

Immigration Acts (1921 & 1924): Emergency Quota Act (1921)

and National Origins Act (1924) severely limited immigration,

especially from S/E Europe and banned most Asians.

Prohibition / Volstead Act (1919): 18th Amendment; Volstead Act

enforced it; led to rise of organized crime (Capone), speakeasies,

bootlegging.

Scopes Trial (1925): TN teacher tried for teaching evolution; Bryan

vs. Darrow; modernism vs. fundamentalism; Scopes convicted, later

overturned.

Adkins v. Children's Hospital (1923): SCOTUS struck down

minimum wage for women; reversed Muller; women now "equal" so

not protected.

Economy, Culture & Foreign Policy

Mass Consumption Economy: Assembly line (Ford), installment

buying, advertising, prosperity of 1920s.

Henry Ford / Automobile: Model T, assembly line, $5/day wage;

transformed American life and economy.

Movies & Radio: Mass entertainment; national culture; celebrity;

Hollywood; Jazz Age.

Harding Corruption: Teapot Dome scandal (oil reserves leased to

private companies for bribes).

Washington Naval Conference (1921–22): Limited warship

tonnage; attempted arms control.

Kellogg-Briand Pact (1928): 62 nations outlawed war as foreign

policy; no enforcement mechanism → useless.

Dawes Plan (1924): U.S. loans to Germany → Germany pays

reparations → Britain/France pay war debts → circular and fragile.

Hawley-Smoot Tariff (1930): Highest tariff in U.S. history; caused

other nations to retaliate → worsened Depression globally.

📝 Causes of the Great Depression

Overproduction in industry & agriculture

📝 Stock market speculation on margin

Weak banking system (bank runs)

📝 Uneven wealth distribution

Hawley-Smoot Tariff killed trade

Buying on credit / consumer debt

Weak farm economy throughout 1920s

Stock Market Crash (Oct. 29, 1929 — "Black Tuesday")

Hoover's Response: Believed in "rugged individualism" and voluntary action; opposed direct federal relief; created RFC (too little, too late); Bonus

Army scandal hurt his image.

📝 UNIT 13: THE NEW DEAL

FDR's Programs (The 3 R's)

3 R's: Relief (immediate help), Recovery (long-term economic),

Reform (prevent future Depression).

FDR's Inaugural (1933): "Only thing we have to fear is fear itself";

restored confidence; promised action.

Bank Holiday: Closed all banks; inspected; only sound ones

reopened; restored confidence in banking system.

Glass-Steagall Act (1933): Separated commercial & investment

banking; created FDIC (insured deposits).

TVA (Tennessee Valley Authority): Federal dams/power in

Tennessee Valley; provided jobs & electricity to rural South.

Social Security Act (1935): Retirement pensions, unemployment

insurance, aid for disabled/children; cornerstone of New Deal.

SEC (Securities & Exchange Commission): Regulated stock

market to prevent future crashes.

WPA (Works Progress Administration): Created millions of

jobs building public works, plus arts/theater/literacy programs.

Agricultural Adjustment Act: Paid farmers to reduce production

→ raise food prices; ruled unconstitutional (1936).

Fair Labor Standards Act (1938): Minimum wage, max 40-hour

work week, banned child labor.

📝 New Deal Politics

20th Amendment (1933): Moved inauguration from March to

January ("Lame Duck" amendment).

21st Amendment (1933): Repealed Prohibition.

Election of 1936: FDR won 46/48 states; New Deal coalition: labor,

immigrants, Black voters (shifted from Republicans), South, farmers.

Court-Packing Scheme (1937): FDR tried to add 6 justices to

Supreme Court after it struck down New Deal programs; Congress

rejected it; hurt FDR's political standing but Court soon stopped

striking down New Deal laws.

📝 3/22/26, 2:55 PM

Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide

file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html

6/7

📝 UNIT 14: HEADING TOWARD WWII

Neutrality & Isolationism

Good Neighbor Policy (1933): FDR pledged not to intervene in

Latin American affairs; improved hemispheric relations.

Neutrality Acts (1935, 36, 37): Banned arms sales/loans to

warring nations; reflected isolationist sentiment; tried to avoid WWI

mistakes.

FDR's Quarantine Speech (1937): Suggested "quarantining"

aggressor nations; public backlash → FDR backed off.

Munich Conference (1938): Britain & France gave Hitler

Sudetenland to avoid war ("appeasement"); only delayed the war.

Pro vs. Anti Isolation: Isolationists (America First, Charles

Lindbergh): stay out. Interventionists: help Allies before U.S. is

attacked.

📝 Steps Toward War

Cash and Carry (1939): Allowed arms sales if buyers paid cash and

transported themselves; helped Allies (Britain had the navy).

Destroyer Deal (1940): FDR gave Britain 50 old destroyers for

leases on British bases; bypassed Congress; shifted away from

neutrality.

Lend-Lease (1941): U.S. could lend/lease weapons to any nation

whose defense was vital to U.S.; effectively ended neutrality; "arsenal

of democracy."

Atlantic Charter (1941): FDR & Churchill's joint statement of war

aims; self-determination, free trade, collective security; basis for UN.

Roosevelt's War Message (Dec. 8, 1941): "A date which will live

in infamy"; asked Congress to declare war after Pearl Harbor.

📝 UNIT 15: WORLD WAR II (EARLY)

U.S. Entry

ABC-1 Agreement (1941): U.S.-Britain secret military planning;

"Europe First" strategy agreed upon; defeat Germany before Japan.

Attack on Pearl Harbor (Dec. 7, 1941): Japanese surprise attack

on U.S. naval base in Hawaii; 2,400 Americans killed; U.S. declared

war on Japan; Germany then declared war on U.S.

Executive Order 9066 (1942): FDR ordered internment of

120,000 Japanese Americans (2/3 U.S. citizens) in camps; violation of

civil liberties; upheld in Korematsu v. U.S.; later condemned and

reparations paid in 1988.

📝 Key Comparison

Articles vs. Constitution

Weak central → strong central gov't

Federalists vs. Anti-Fed

Strong central vs. states' rights

Hamilton vs. Jefferson

Industry/bank vs. agrarian/strict

Lincoln vs. Congress (Reconst.)

Lenient 10% vs. harsh Wade-Davis

TR vs. Wilson Progressivism

Regulate trusts vs. bust trusts

Isolationism vs. Intervention

1930s debate: stay out vs. help Allies

📌 EXAM REMINDERS: The exam includes Images, Maps, Charts, and Document Text. Practice identifying visual primary sources. DBQ tip: use

HAPP (Historical context, Audience, Purpose, Point of view) for document analysis. SAQ tip: answer all parts (a, b, c) with specific evidence. Exam

Date: March 27, 2026 — 60 objective + 1 DBQ + 1 SAQ.

Honors U.S. History Study Guide — Generated by Mojo Jojo 🐵 | Good luck rx!

📝 3/22/26, 2:55 PM

Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide

file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html

7/7
units.rawText:
📜 Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide
Exam: March 27, 2026 | 60 Objective Questions + DBQ + SAQ | Created by Mojo Jojo 🐵
Columbian Exchange & Early Exploration
Columbian Exchange: Transfer of plants, animals, diseases, culture
between Old & New Worlds after 1492. Devastated Native populations
via disease (smallpox).
Treaty of Tordesillas (1494): Spain & Portugal divided New
World; Spain got most of Americas.
Black Legend: Propaganda portraying Spanish as uniquely cruel to
Natives; used by English/Dutch to justify their own colonization.
Protestant Reformation: Led by Martin Luther; challenged
Catholic Church; motivated religious migration to Americas.
Spanish Armada (1588): England defeated Spain's fleet → opened
Atlantic to English colonization.
Joint Stock Company: Investors pool risk/profit; funded Virginia
Company → Jamestown (1607).
Colonial Founding & Development
Barbados Slave Codes (1661): First formal slave laws; brought to
Carolina; defined enslaved as property.
Mayflower Compact (1620): First self-government doc in
America; Pilgrims agreed to majority rule.
"City upon a Hill" (1630): Winthrop's speech; Puritan vision of
model Christian community in Massachusetts.
Anglo-Powhatan Wars (1610–1646): Conflicts between English
settlers and Powhatan Confederacy in Virginia.
King Philip's War (1675–76): Wampanoag (Metacom/King Philip)
vs. colonists; bloodiest colonial war per capita; Natives lost.
New England Confederation (1643): First attempt at colonial
cooperation for defense against Natives & Dutch.
Colonial Economy & Society
Triangular Trade: Manufactured goods → Africa → slaves →
Americas → raw materials → Europe.
Bacon's Rebellion (1676): Virginia frontiersmen vs. Governor
Berkeley; burned Jamestown; led planters to prefer enslaved Africans
over indentured servants.
Salutary Neglect: Britain loosely enforced trade laws → colonies
developed self-governance & smuggling habits.
New France: French colonies in Canada; focused on fur trade; good
Native relations; few settlers compared to British.
Road to the French & Indian War
Ohio River Valley: France & Britain competed for control →
sparked French & Indian War (1754–63).
Albany Congress (1754): Franklin's Plan of Union proposed
colonial cooperation; rejected by colonies & Crown.
Battle of Quebec (1759): British victory; decisive battle ending
French power in North America.
Proclamation of 1763: Britain forbade colonists from settling west
of Appalachians; colonists furious → fueled resentment.
Mercantilism: Colonies exist to enrich the mother country; raw
materials out, manufactured goods in; colonies can only trade with
Britain.
Stamp Act (1765): First direct tax on colonists (paper goods);
colonists: "No taxation without representation!" → Stamp Act
Congress formed.
Boston Massacre (1770): British soldiers killed 5 colonists; used as
propaganda by Patriots (Paul Revere's engraving).
Boston Tea Party (1773): Sons of Liberty dumped 342 chests of tea
→ Parliament passed Intolerable Acts.
Intolerable Acts (1774): Closed Boston Harbor, revoked MA self-
government, forced housing of troops → united colonies against
Britain.
First Continental Congress (1774): Delegates met; agreed to
boycott British goods; not yet seeking independence.
Battle of Bunker Hill (1775): Technically British win but costly;
showed colonists could fight.
Olive Branch Petition (1775): Last attempt at reconciliation with
King George; rejected.
Common Sense (1776): Thomas Paine; argued for independence in
plain language; widely read.
Declaration of Independence (1776): Jefferson; natural rights
(Locke); listed grievances; "all men are created equal."
Battle of Saratoga (1777): Turning point; convinced France to ally
with Americans.
Treaty of Paris (1783): Britain recognized U.S. independence; U.S.
got land to Mississippi River.
British strengths: trained army, navy, Loyalist support, $$$. Colonial
strengths: home turf, guerrilla tactics, Washington's leadership, French
alliance.
Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide
file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html
1/7
Articles of Confederation → Constitution
Articles of Confederation: 1st U.S. gov't. Strengths: Northwest
Ordinance, Land Ordinance. Weaknesses: No power to tax, no
executive, no regulate commerce, required unanimous votes to amend.
Land Ordinance of 1785: Divided NW Territory into townships for
sale; funded public education (Section 16).
Northwest Ordinance (1787): Outlined path to statehood; banned
slavery in NW Territory; protected civil liberties.
Great Compromise: Bicameral Congress: Senate (2/state) + House
(population-based).
Three-Fifths Compromise: Enslaved counted as 3/5 of a person
for representation & taxation.
Electoral College: Compromise for indirect presidential election;
small states get more weight.
Federalists: Supported Constitution; strong central government;
Hamilton, Madison, Jay.
Anti-Federalists: Opposed Constitution; feared tyranny; wanted Bill
of Rights first.
Federalist Papers: 85 essays by Hamilton, Madison, Jay defending
Constitution. Key: #10 (factions), #51 (checks & balances).
Bill of Rights (1791): First 10 Amendments; added to win Anti-
Federalist support for ratification.
Hamilton's Economic Program
Financial Plan: Assumed state debts, created national bank, tariffs
for industry, excise taxes.
Bank of the United States: Hamilton's plan; Jefferson opposed
(strict vs. loose interpretation of Constitution).
Whiskey Rebellion (1794): Farmers refused to pay whiskey tax;
Washington sent militia → showed federal power.
Washington & Adams Era
Neutrality Proclamation (1793): Washington stayed neutral in
France vs. Britain war.
Jay's Treaty (1795): Settled issues with Britain; controversial;
critics said U.S. gave too much.
Washington's Farewell Address (1796): Warned against political
Alien & Sedition Acts (1798): Adams; extended citizenship wait;
made it illegal to criticize government → Democratic-Republicans
outraged.
VA & KY Resolutions (1798–99): Madison & Jefferson; states can
nullify unconstitutional federal laws (nullification theory).
Democratic-Republican
Strong central gov't
States' rights
Pro-British
Pro-French
Commerce & manufacturing
Election of 1800: "Revolution of 1800" — peaceful transfer of
power; Jefferson beat Adams; Burr tie led to 12th Amendment.
Marbury v. Madison (1803): Marshall established judicial review
— Supreme Court can strike down laws. Most important SCOTUS
case.
Louisiana Purchase (1803): Doubled U.S. size for $15M from
Napoleon; Jefferson stretched strict interpretation.
Embargo Act (1807): Jefferson banned all exports to hurt Britain &
France; backfired — hurt American economy.
Battle of New Orleans (1815): Jackson's great victory —
technically after Treaty of Ghent ended War of 1812.
Hartford Convention (1814–15): Federalists opposed War of
1812; proposed amendments; ended their party when war ended well.
Era of Good Feelings & Monroe
Tariff of 1816: First protective tariff; protected American industry
from British competition.
American System (Clay): Protective tariffs + national bank +
internal improvements (roads/canals).
Missouri Compromise (1820): Missouri = slave, Maine = free;
36°30' line divided future states.
Monroe Doctrine (1823): Western Hemisphere closed to European
colonization; U.S. would not interfere in Europe.
Election of 1824 / Corrupt Bargain: No majority → House
decided; Clay gave votes to J.Q. Adams; Adams made Clay Sec. of
State → "corrupt bargain."
Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide
file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html
2/7
Election of 1828: Jackson won; "Age of the Common Man";
expanded voting rights (white men only).
Spoils System: Rewarding political supporters with government jobs
("rotation in office").
Nullification Crisis (1832): SC nullified Tariffs of 1828 & 1832
("Tariff of Abominations"); Jackson threatened force; Compromise of
1833 resolved it.
Bank War: Jackson vetoed recharter of Bank of U.S.; called it
"monster" favoring the rich; withdrew fed funds to state "pet banks."
Indian Removal Act (1830): Forced Native tribes west of
Mississippi; Trail of Tears (1838) — Cherokee forced march; ~4,000
died.
Cultural & Reform Movements
Erie Canal (1825): Connected Great Lakes to NYC; made NY
commercial hub; transportation revolution.
Second Great Awakening: Religious revival; inspired reform
movements (abolitionism, temperance, women's rights).
Seneca Falls Convention (1848): First women's rights
convention; Declaration of Sentiments ("all men and women are
created equal"); led by Stanton & Mott.
Free Soil Party (1848): Opposed spread of slavery to new
territories; "Free Soil, Free Speech, Free Labor, Free Men."
Abolition Movement: Garrison (The Liberator), Frederick Douglass
(escaped slave/speaker), Harriet Tubman (Underground Railroad).
Slavery Justifications: Necessary Evil → Peculiar Institution
→ Positive Good (evolution of Southern defense of slavery).
Manifest Destiny & Mexican-American War
Whig Party / John Tyler: Formed against Jackson; Tyler (Whig
VP) vetoed Whig agenda → expelled from party.
Manifest Destiny: Belief God destined Americans to expand across
continent; justified expansion/displacement of Natives.
Mexican-American War (1846–48): Texas annexation dispute;
U.S. gained CA, NM, AZ, NV, UT, CO from Mexico (Mexican Cession).
Wilmot Proviso (1846): Proposed banning slavery in any territory
gained from Mexico; passed House, failed Senate; intensified sectional
crisis.
Compromise of 1850: CA = free state; popular sovereignty in
NM/UT; stronger Fugitive Slave Law; ended DC slave trade.
Ostend Manifesto (1854): Secret plan to buy/seize Cuba for
slavery; leaked → outrage in North.
Gadsden Purchase (1853): Bought southern AZ/NM from Mexico
for railroad route.
Popular Sovereignty: Let settlers in each territory vote on slavery;
proposed by Douglas; pleased neither side.
Underground Railroad: Network of safe houses helping enslaved
escape to North/Canada; Harriet Tubman made 19 trips.
Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854): Repealed Missouri Compromise;
popular sovereignty in KS & NE → "Bleeding Kansas."
Uncle Tom's Cabin (1852): Harriet Beecher Stowe; novel
humanizing enslaved people; radicalized Northern opinion against
slavery.
Bleeding Kansas (1856): Pro- & anti-slavery settlers fought in
Kansas; violence previewed Civil War.
Dred Scott Decision (1857): SCOTUS: enslaved people = property,
not citizens; Congress cannot ban slavery in territories; enraged
North.
Harper's Ferry (1859): John Brown's raid on federal arsenal to
spark slave revolt; captured, executed; made him martyr in North,
villain in South.
Election of 1860: Lincoln won without a single Southern electoral
vote; South saw it as signal to secede.
Southern Secession: SC left first; 11 states formed Confederate
States of America; said states had right to leave.
Strengths, Strategies & Key Events
North
South
Larger population, industry
Defensive war, home turf
RR network, navy
$$$, more factories
"King Cotton" diplomacy
King Wheat (food for Europe)
Fighting spirit/morale
Border States: Slave states that stayed in Union (MO, KY, MD, DE);
Lincoln kept them through careful politics.
Anaconda Plan: Union strategy: blockade South, control
Mississippi, squeeze Confederate economy.
Draft / NYC Draft Riots (1863): Poor workers (esp. Irish
immigrants) rioted against draft; could pay $300 to avoid service —
"rich man's war, poor man's fight."
Key Documents & Battles
Emancipation Proclamation (1863): Freed enslaved in
Confederate states only; war aim now included ending slavery;
prevented European recognition of Confederacy.
Gettysburg Address (1863): Lincoln's speech redefining war as
fight for equality and democracy.
Battle of Antietam (1862): Bloodiest single day; tactical draw but
Union "victory" → gave Lincoln cover to issue Emancipation
Proclamation.
Battle of Gettysburg (1863): Turning point in East; Lee's invasion
of North repelled; 50,000+ casualties over 3 days.
Battle of Vicksburg (1863): Union captured last Confederate
stronghold on Mississippi → split Confederacy in two.
Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide
file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html
3/7
Plans & Politics
Lincoln's 10% Plan: South could return when 10% swore loyalty
oath; moderate approach.
Johnson's Requirements: Similar to Lincoln's but added large
landowner pardon requests; lenient toward ex-Confederates.
Wade-Davis Bill (1864): Required 50% loyalty oath; pocket-vetoed
by Lincoln; showed Radical Republican desire for harsher terms.
Military Reconstruction Act (1867): Divided South into 5
military districts; required new state constitutions + ratify 14th
Amendment to rejoin.
Moderates vs. Radicals: Moderate Republicans wanted quick
reunion; Radicals (Sumner, Stevens) wanted to protect Black rights &
punish South.
Amendments, Resistance & End
Black Codes: Southern laws restricting freedmen's rights; forced
labor, limited movement; essentially re-enslaved many.
Freedmen's Bureau: Federal agency providing food, education,
legal aid to formerly enslaved people.
13th Amendment (1865): Abolished slavery.
14th Amendment (1868): Citizenship for all born in U.S.; equal
protection; due process.
15th Amendment (1870): Cannot deny vote based on race.
Redeemer Governments: Southern Democrats regained control;
ended Reconstruction reforms.
Jim Crow Laws: Racial segregation laws in South; "separate but
equal."
KKK: Founded 1865; terrorized Black voters, Republicans, freedmen
through violence.
Plessy v. Ferguson (1896): SCOTUS upheld "separate but equal"
→ legalized segregation for 58 years until Brown v. Board.
Politics & Reform
Waving the Bloody Shirt: Republicans blamed Democrats for Civil
War to win elections.
Compromise of 1877: Hayes became president; federal troops left
South → ended Reconstruction.
Pendleton Act (1883): Created civil service exam; ended patronage
for federal jobs; merit-based hiring.
Thomas Nast: Political cartoonist; exposed Boss Tweed/Tammany
Hall corruption; invented Republican elephant & Democratic donkey.
Grant Admin Corruption: Crédit Mobilier, Whiskey Ring scandals;
though Grant himself likely not corrupt.
Interstate Commerce Act (1887): First federal regulation of
railroads; created ICC.
Sherman Anti-Trust Act (1890): First federal law breaking up
monopolies; rarely enforced until TR.
Wabash v. Illinois (1886): States cannot regulate interstate
railroad rates → need for federal regulation.
Industry, Labor & Immigration
Vertical Integration (Carnegie): Control all stages of production
(steel: mines → mills → railroads).
Horizontal Integration (Rockefeller): Control same stage across
industry (buy out all oil refineries).
Old Immigrants: Pre-1880, W. Europe (British, German, Irish);
Protestant; assimilated more easily.
New Immigrants: Post-1880, S/E Europe (Italian, Polish, Jewish,
Russian); Catholic/Jewish; faced more discrimination; Nativist
backlash.
Homestead Strike (1892): Carnegie Steel vs. workers; Pinkerton
agents hired; workers lost; set back unions.
Pullman Strike (1894): Nationwide railroad strike; Cleveland sent
federal troops; Eugene Debs arrested → showed government sided
with business.
Homestead Act (1862): 160 acres free to settlers who farmed 5
years; drew millions West.
Railroad Development: Transcontinental RR (1869) connected
nation; Chinese & Irish labor; transformed economy.
Dawes Severalty Act (1887): Broke up tribal lands; gave Native
families 160 acres; destroyed tribal culture; took 90M acres from
Natives.
Farmers' Problems: Falling prices, railroad monopolies, debt; led
to Grange movement and Populist Party.
The Grange / Farmers' Alliance: Farmers' organizations; pushed
for railroad regulation, currency reform.
Populist Party & Election of 1896
Populist (People's) Party: Demanded: direct election of senators,
graduated income tax, government ownership of railroads, silver
coinage.
Election of 1896: Bryan (D/Populist) vs. McKinley (R); Bryan's
"Cross of Gold" speech demanded silver coinage to inflate prices and
help debtors; McKinley won; end of Populism.
Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide
file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html
4/7
Isolation → Imperialism: Mahan's "sea power" theory; Social
Darwinism; need for markets; influenced by European empires.
Annexation of Hawaii (1898): American planters overthrew
Queen Liliuokalani; Cleveland refused annexation; McKinley annexed
it.
Spanish-American War (1898): USS Maine explosion; yellow
journalism; Cuba/Philippines; "Splendid Little War"; U.S. became
imperial power.
Treaty of Paris (1898): Spain gave U.S. Philippines, Guam, Puerto
Rico for $20M; Cuba became independent (nominally).
Platt Amendment (1901): U.S. retained right to intervene in Cuba;
Cuba could not sign treaties without U.S. approval → virtual
protectorate.
Pacific & Latin American Policy
Open Door Notes (1899–1900): Secretary Hay; demanded equal
trading rights in China for all nations; protected China from full
colonization.
Philippine Rebellion (1899–1902): Filipinos (led by Aguinaldo)
fought U.S. occupation; brutal war; contradicted American ideals.
Roosevelt Corollary (1904): Extension of Monroe Doctrine; U.S.
("international police power").
Panama Canal (1914): TR supported Panamanian revolution vs.
Colombia; U.S. built canal; transformed global trade.
Roosevelt's Progressivism
Progressive Movement: Reason: fix problems of industrialization.
Goals: break monopolies, protect consumers, expand democracy.
Supporters: middle class, muckrakers, women.
Square Deal: TR's program: control corporations, consumer
protection, conservation of natural resources (3 C's).
The Jungle (1906): Upton Sinclair exposed meatpacking industry
horrors → Meat Inspection Act & Pure Food & Drug Act.
Anthracite Coal Strike (1902): TR mediated; first time gov't sided
with workers (sort of); both sides arbitrated.
Northern Securities Case (1904): TR busted railroad monopoly
using Sherman Anti-Trust Act → "trust-busting."
Newlands Act (1902): Federal money from land sales used for
irrigation in West; conservation effort.
Muller v. Oregon (1908): SCOTUS upheld law limiting women's
work hours; used sociological data (Brandeis Brief).
Taft, Wilson & Progressive Amendments
TR vs. Taft: TR hand-picked Taft; Taft disappointed TR with
conservative moves; TR ran again as Bull Moose.
New Nationalism (TR) vs. New Freedom (Wilson): TR:
regulate big business but keep it. Wilson: break up all trusts, restore
competition.
Bull Moose Party (1912): TR's third party; split Republican vote →
Wilson won.
Wilson's "Triple Wall of Privilege": Attacked tariffs, banks, trusts
via: Underwood Tariff (lowered rates), Federal Reserve Act (central
banking), Clayton Anti-Trust Act (strengthened Sherman).
16th Amendment: Federal income tax.
17th Amendment: Direct election of senators (previously by state
legislatures).
18th Amendment: Prohibition (ban on alcohol).
19th Amendment: Women's suffrage.
U.S. Entry & Key Events
Lusitania (1915): German U-boat sank British liner killing 1,198
incl. 128 Americans; outraged U.S. public.
Sussex Pledge (1916): Germany promised not to sink passenger
ships without warning after Sussex attack.
Fourteen Points (1918): Wilson's peace plan: freedom of seas, self-
determination, League of Nations; idealistic.
Schenck v. United States (1919): SCOTUS upheld limits on free
speech during wartime; "clear and present danger" test; upheld
Espionage Act.
Treaty of Versailles (1919): Blamed Germany (War Guilt Clause);
$33B reparations; stripped German territory; humiliated Germany →
seeds of WWII.
League of Nations: Wilson's pet project; Senate rejected U.S.
membership (Lodge Reservations); Article X concerned U.S.
sovereignty.
Lodge Reservations: Senator Lodge's conditions for joining
League; mainly about preserving Congressional war powers; Wilson
refused compromise.
Home Front & Aftermath
Committee on Public Information: Gov't propaganda bureau;
stirred anti-German sentiment.
Espionage & Sedition Acts: Criminalized opposition to the war;
suppressed civil liberties.
Great Migration: Black Americans moved North for industrial jobs;
escaped Jim Crow.
Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide
file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html
5/7
Red Scare (1919–20): Fear of communist revolution after Russian
Revolution; Palmer Raids; Sacco & Vanzetti.
Immigration Acts (1921 & 1924): Emergency Quota Act (1921)
and National Origins Act (1924) severely limited immigration,
especially from S/E Europe and banned most Asians.
Prohibition / Volstead Act (1919): 18th Amendment; Volstead Act
enforced it; led to rise of organized crime (Capone), speakeasies,
bootlegging.
Scopes Trial (1925): TN teacher tried for teaching evolution; Bryan
vs. Darrow; modernism vs. fundamentalism; Scopes convicted, later
overturned.
Adkins v. Children's Hospital (1923): SCOTUS struck down
minimum wage for women; reversed Muller; women now "equal" so
not protected.
Economy, Culture & Foreign Policy
Mass Consumption Economy: Assembly line (Ford), installment
buying, advertising, prosperity of 1920s.
Henry Ford / Automobile: Model T, assembly line, $5/day wage;
transformed American life and economy.
Movies & Radio: Mass entertainment; national culture; celebrity;
Hollywood; Jazz Age.
Harding Corruption: Teapot Dome scandal (oil reserves leased to
private companies for bribes).
Washington Naval Conference (1921–22): Limited warship
tonnage; attempted arms control.
Kellogg-Briand Pact (1928): 62 nations outlawed war as foreign
policy; no enforcement mechanism → useless.
Dawes Plan (1924): U.S. loans to Germany → Germany pays
reparations → Britain/France pay war debts → circular and fragile.
Hawley-Smoot Tariff (1930): Highest tariff in U.S. history; caused
other nations to retaliate → worsened Depression globally.
Overproduction in industry & agriculture
Weak banking system (bank runs)
Hawley-Smoot Tariff killed trade
Buying on credit / consumer debt
Weak farm economy throughout 1920s
Stock Market Crash (Oct. 29, 1929 — "Black Tuesday")
Hoover's Response: Believed in "rugged individualism" and voluntary action; opposed direct federal relief; created RFC (too little, too late); Bonus
Army scandal hurt his image.
FDR's Programs (The 3 R's)
3 R's: Relief (immediate help), Recovery (long-term economic),
Reform (prevent future Depression).
FDR's Inaugural (1933): "Only thing we have to fear is fear itself";
restored confidence; promised action.
Bank Holiday: Closed all banks; inspected; only sound ones
reopened; restored confidence in banking system.
Glass-Steagall Act (1933): Separated commercial & investment
banking; created FDIC (insured deposits).
TVA (Tennessee Valley Authority): Federal dams/power in
Tennessee Valley; provided jobs & electricity to rural South.
Social Security Act (1935): Retirement pensions, unemployment
insurance, aid for disabled/children; cornerstone of New Deal.
SEC (Securities & Exchange Commission): Regulated stock
market to prevent future crashes.
WPA (Works Progress Administration): Created millions of
jobs building public works, plus arts/theater/literacy programs.
Agricultural Adjustment Act: Paid farmers to reduce production
→ raise food prices; ruled unconstitutional (1936).
Fair Labor Standards Act (1938): Minimum wage, max 40-hour
work week, banned child labor.
20th Amendment (1933): Moved inauguration from March to
January ("Lame Duck" amendment).
21st Amendment (1933): Repealed Prohibition.
Election of 1936: FDR won 46/48 states; New Deal coalition: labor,
immigrants, Black voters (shifted from Republicans), South, farmers.
Court-Packing Scheme (1937): FDR tried to add 6 justices to
Supreme Court after it struck down New Deal programs; Congress
rejected it; hurt FDR's political standing but Court soon stopped
striking down New Deal laws.
Honors U.S. History — 2nd Trimester Exam Study Guide
file:///tmp/ush_study_guide.html
6/7
Neutrality & Isolationism
Good Neighbor Policy (1933): FDR pledged not to intervene in
Latin American affairs; improved hemispheric relations.
Neutrality Acts (1935, 36, 37): Banned arms sales/loans to
warring nations; reflected isolationist sentiment; tried to avoid WWI
mistakes.
FDR's Quarantine Speech (1937): Suggested "quarantining"
aggressor nations; public backlash → FDR backed off.
Munich Conference (1938): Britain & France gave Hitler
Sudetenland to avoid war ("appeasement"); only delayed the war.
Pro vs. Anti Isolation: Isolationists (America First, Charles
Lindbergh): stay out. Interventionists: help Allies before U.S. is
attacked.
Cash and Carry (1939): Allowed arms sales if buyers paid cash and
transported themselves; helped Allies (Britain had the navy).
Destroyer Deal (1940): FDR gave Britain 50 old destroyers for
leases on British bases; bypassed Congress; shifted away from
neutrality.
Lend-Lease (1941): U.S. could lend/lease weapons to any nation
whose defense was vital to U.S.; effectively ended neutrality; "arsenal
of democracy."
Atlantic Charter (1941): FDR & Churchill's joint statement of war
aims; self-determination, free trade, collective security; basis for UN.
Roosevelt's War Message (Dec. 8, 1941): "A date which will live
in infamy"; asked Congress to declare war after Pearl Harbor.
U.S. Entry
ABC-1 Agreement (1941): U.S.-Britain secret military planning;
"Europe First" strategy agreed upon; defeat Germany before Japan.
Attack on Pearl Harbor (Dec. 7, 1941): Japanese surprise attack
on U.S. naval base in Hawaii; 2,400 Americans killed; U.S. declared
war on Japan; Germany then declared war on U.S.
Executive Order 9066 (1942): FDR ordered internment of
120,000 Japanese Americans (2/3 U.S. citizens) in camps; violation of
civil liberties; upheld in Korematsu v. U.S.; later condemned and
reparations paid in 1988.
Articles vs. Constitution
Weak central → strong central gov't
Federalists vs. Anti-Fed
Strong central vs. states' rights
Hamilton vs. Jefferson
Industry/bank vs. agrarian/strict
Lincoln vs. Congress (Reconst.)
Lenient 10% vs. harsh Wade-Davis
TR vs. Wilson Progressivism
Regulate trusts vs. bust trusts
Isolationism vs. Intervention
1930s debate: stay out vs. help Allies

7/7
